planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall; Aurea lemon balm establishes readily in cool, moist conditions. In zone 9 plant September–November for fall establishment or January–March for spring. The golden variegation is most intense in spring and fall; summer heat may cause the foliage to revert to mostly green. Divide crowded clumps every 2–3 years in early spring.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; Lemon Balm Aurea (also known as Golden Lemon Balm or Variegated Lemon Balm) is a cultivar of common lemon balm distinguished by brilliant gold-splashed, yellow-green variegated foliage in spring and early fall — the leaves are irregularly mottled with golden-yellow against the base green, giving the plant an ornamental quality entirely absent from plain lemon balm. The lemon fragrance is identical to the species, and the leaves have the same culinary and herbal uses — teas, desserts, liqueurs, and salads — with the added benefit of attractive foliage that makes this herb garden-worthy as an ornamental. In hot zone 9 summers the gold variegation tends to fade to mostly green as the plant responds to heat stress; for the best color effect, grow in partial shade and harvest the brightest spring leaves for culinary use. The plant spreads by self-sowing in fertile soils; deadhead before seed set to prevent naturalizing.
sources: Cornell University Herb Garden — Melissa officinalis Cultivars
Royal Horticultural Society — Melissa officinalis 'Aurea' Plant Finder
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lemon Balm Herb Profile

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 start January–February and transplant March–April. Dr. Wyche's Yellow is an indeterminate variety that continues producing through the Gulf Coast season until heat causes fruit set to slow in July–August; a second wave often appears in fall. Stake or cage plants early as the large fruits on vigorous vines require substantial support.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun with consistent moisture; Dr. Wyche's Yellow is a large, orange-gold beefsteak tomato developed by Dr. John Wyche of Hugo, Oklahoma and preserved by Seed Savers Exchange. The fruits are massive — typically 1–2 pounds — with a brilliant golden-orange interior and exterior, strikingly beautiful when sliced to reveal the gel-filled seed chambers set against golden flesh. The flavor is exceptionally rich, complex, and sweet with low acidity — the orange and yellow pigments indicate high beta-carotene content and lower lycopene compared to red tomatoes. Dr. Wyche's Yellow is prized for Caprese salads, sliced tomato presentations, and fresh eating where the warm gold color provides visual contrast. It is not a cooking tomato — the delicate flavor and high water content are best appreciated raw. Like most large heirloom beefsteaks, it has some susceptibility to cracking and blossom-end rot; maintain consistent watering and calcium levels.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Dr. Wyche's Yellow Tomato Heritage Variety
Oklahoma State University Extension — Heirloom Tomato Varieties
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Large Heirloom Tomato Types

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 start January and transplant March–April. Pruden's Purple is one of the earliest-maturing large pink beefsteak heirlooms, producing harvestable fruits 2–3 weeks before Brandywine in comparable conditions — a significant advantage in zone 9 where spring heat arrives early. The indeterminate vines are vigorous and productive; provide large cages or stakes.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Pruden's Purple is an indeterminate, potato-leaf heirloom beefsteak tomato producing very large (1–2 pound), pink-to-purplish-red fruits with a rich, complex, low-acid flavor — considered by many tomato enthusiasts to be equal or superior to Brandywine in flavor with the significant advantage of earlier maturity (65–78 days versus 85–100 days for Brandywine). The large, deeply lobed fruits have the classic thick-walled, few-seeded quality of potato-leaf beefsteak types. Pruden's Purple is particularly valuable in zone 9 where the spring tomato season closes when heat arrives in late May–June — the earlier maturity allows a full harvest before the season ends. The thin skin characteristic of large pink beefsteaks requires careful watering consistency to prevent cracking; maintain steady moisture throughout fruit development.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Pruden's Purple Tomato Heritage Variety
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Large Pink Beefsteak Types
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Potato-Leaf Heirloom Tomatoes

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant October–March for best establishment. Kishu mandarin is cold-hardy to about 24°F, making it more vulnerable to hard freezes than navel oranges — protect young trees in zone 9a winters with frost cloth or heat lamps when temperatures drop below 28°F. Trees are self-fertile and begin bearing within 2–4 years.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–7.0) soil; Kishu mandarin (Kishu mikan) is a tiny Japanese mandarin producing small, round, intensely sweet, completely seedless fruits — typically 1–1.5 inches in diameter, roughly the size of a large grape. The combination of total seedlessness and extraordinary sweetness makes Kishu the darling of high-end specialty markets, where the small size makes it a perfect one-bite citrus snack. The paper-thin skin peels effortlessly in a single piece, and the flavor is intensely sweet with rich tropical complexity that surpasses most commercial mandarins. Trees are compact and manageable at 6–10 feet, well-suited to zone 9 and container growing. Kishu ripens in December–January, slightly earlier than most clementine-type mandarins. Although small trees, they bear prolifically and a single established tree can produce hundreds of fruits. Avoid planting in frost pockets where cold air settles.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Kishu Mandarin
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Citrus Varieties for Florida
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Citrus kinokuni

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–November or January–March. Ginger Mint spreads by underground runners like all Mentha species — contain in pots or use a buried barrier to prevent invasive spreading in garden beds. In zone 9 the plant thrives in cool seasons (fall through spring) and goes partially dormant in peak summer heat.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; Ginger Mint (Mentha x gracilis) is a naturally occurring hybrid mint producing slender, red-stemmed plants with smooth, bright green to slightly variegated leaves that carry a distinctive ginger-mint aroma — a warm, spicy, gingery note overlaid on the base mint freshness, quite unlike spearmint or peppermint. The flavor is used in teas, cold drinks, fruit salads, and Southeast Asian cooking where ginger notes complement the mint. Some variegated selections ('Variegata') display gold-streaked leaves that add ornamental interest. Like all culinary mints, Ginger Mint spreads by underground runners and should be contained to prevent it from colonizing adjacent plantings. The red stems are distinctive and make Ginger Mint identifiable even at a distance. In zone 9 it grows vigorously in spring and fall and benefits from afternoon shade to prevent leaf scorch in summer.
sources: Cornell University Herb Garden — Mentha x gracilis
University of California Cooperative Extension — Culinary Mint Varieties
Nichols Garden Nursery — Specialty Mint Selection Guide
